Rapyd提供了一个基于API的“金融科技即服务”平台，涵盖支付，银行服务，欺诈保护等内容，已筹集了3亿美元，该资金将用于CEO和联合创始人Arik Shtilman的采访中扩大其团队，建立更多技术（下一个：扩展的欺诈ID服务和更广阔的市场）并进行有选择的收购。
 Rapyd的客户群目前约有5,000家，其中包括交易市场（劳动力交易市场和商品交易市场），电子商务业务，其他类型的贷方以及任何可能希望将交易或新金融服务纳入其更广泛产品范围的业务。 Shtilman表示，目前Rapyd的增长最为强劲，每周约有500名新客户。
  Shtilman证实，这笔资金将达到25亿美元的事后估值。 （就此而言，拉皮德于2019年12月的最后估值为12亿美元。）
 该轮融资是D轮融资，由多产的成长型VC Coatue，Spark Capital，Avid Ventures，FJ Labs和Latitude（所有新的支持者）以及General Catalyst，Oak HC / FT，Tiger Global，Target牵头。全球，耐久资本，塔尔资本和恩特雷资本（所有先前的支持者）也参与了。值得注意的是，其他过去的投资者包括基于API的金融服务领域的另一主要参与者Stripe。
 与其他类别的公司在过去一年中需求激增一样，金融服务，尤其是那些提供能够通过互联网或电话进行在线交易的服务的公司，已被证明是其中最重要的一些。强制性和最常用的。 （也就不足为奇了，因为仍然需要支付账单，仍然需要购买食物和其他物品，所以仍然非常需要借贷等等。）
 这就是很多人所说的“机会主义”加薪，并不是为了保持亮灯或延长跑道，而是因为钱是按良好条件提供给Rapyd的，而且有一些聪明的地方可以用来支付发展业务。
 Shtilman说：“当初筹集资金时，我们并不打算筹集资金，但是当大流行开始时，我们的业务开始蓬勃发展。” “在2020年的三个月内完成2021年增长计划后，现有投资者要求我们扩大规模，超出原有的业务计划。因此，我们认为时机可能适合全球统治。” 
Shtilman在某种程度上（只是部分地）在开玩笑-他有点呆板的交付，我在这里无法完全捕捉到-但是这与初创公司的早期相去甚远，当时“没人愿意投资，因为有人认为这会太难执行了。甚至我们的早期投资者也建议我们关注较小的概念。但是我们认为在全球范围内进行建设是行不通的。从小处着手是不对的。在过去的几年中，解释我们所做的事情的需求几乎消失了。”
 Rapyd在2017年首次营业时（最初是在以色列孵化的）就面临的挑战（和机遇）是全球商业和金融市场高度分散。各个市场中的消费者和企业都有自己喜欢的付款方式和要求，法规不同，所涉及的主要公司因国家而异。
 同时，API长期以来一直是集成和连接的绝佳工具：使用几行代码-并假设您自己的服务也基于代码构建-您可以将服务结合在一起，并引入商品化的功能，而这些功能可能需要很长时间才能构建从头开始，减少工作量和工作量，专注于使您的核心业务更加独特。
  尽管诸如Stripe，Twilio等公司发现了利用API来向更多潜在客户扩展功能世界的机会，但Rapyd真正确定并建立的想法是加载不仅一个，两个或三项服务，但有数百个（甚至数千个）功能加入了这一主张。在那些服务非常复杂的金融科技中，有很多服务供您选择构建内容，如果您的目标是广泛的话，也有大量潜在的客户可以使用它们。
 这个想法很聪明，而且正如Shtilman所指出的那样，与电子商务和金融科技中存在的规模经济非常相符：个体交易在一天结束时是非常增量的，因此将许多人聚集在一起的服务最终可以开始想到有趣的回报。
 当然，这不仅仅是Rapyd所识别和使用的东西。也就是说，该公司现在在市场上有许多竞争对手。
 就在上周，总部位于德国的Mambu以“银行即服务”的理念提供了基于API的服务套件（最新数量为7,000），以总价值超过20亿美元的价格筹集了1.35亿美元。 Stripe是Rapyd的支持者，它还继续扩展并添加了许多服务，远远超出了付款范围。去年，Thought Machine也筹集了大笔资金；特梅诺斯（Temenos）和意大利的埃德拉（Edera）也是这里的佼佼者。 
而且该领域的机会如此之多，甚至吸引了许多新进入者：见证单位Unit，另一个有趣的参与者，于12月在美国脱颖而出，并拥有自己的有趣支持者。
  Shtilman指出：“建立金融基础设施，无论您是小妈妈，流行歌手还是更大的东西，都需要很多东西，如果您想在一个以上的司法管辖区进行销售，则需要很多这些服务。”有关金融科技平台主张中规模和广度的需求。他对竞争也很乐观。
 他说：“雨后它们像蘑菇一样出现。” “但是，如果您没有竞争，那就意味着您没有生意，所以这很好。这意味着有很多需求。但是目前我们是市场领导者。我们认为我们将成为这一领域的AWS。”
  “不同国家的支付格局差异很大。在全球开展业务的公司可能需要接受数百种本地付款方式。 Rapyd的API简化了这种复杂性，目前正在推动我们认为是世界上许多最令人兴奋的公司的发展，” Coatue执行合伙人Kris Fredrickson在一份声明中说。 “我们很荣幸能与Arik和团队合作进行Rapyd旅程的下一阶段。”
note_en:
Rapyd, which provides an API-based “fintech-as-a-service” platform covering payments, banking services, fraud protection and more, has raised $300 million, funding that CEO and co-founder Arik Shtilman said in an interview will be used to expand its team, build out more technology (next up: expanded fraud ID services and a wider marketplace) and make selected acquisitions.
 Rapyd’s customer base now numbers about 5,000 businesses, which includes marketplaces (labor marketplaces and marketplaces for goods), e-commerce businesses, other kinds of lenders and any business that might want to incorporate transactions or new financial services into their wider offerings. Shtilman said that at the moment, Rapyd is seeing its strongest growth yet, onboarding about 500 new customers each week.
  The funding is coming at a $2.5 billion post-money valuation, Shtilman confirmed. (For some context on that, Rapyd was last valued at $1.2 billion in  December 2019.)
 The round is a Series D and is being led by prolific growth-round VC Coatue, with Spark Capital, Avid Ventures, FJ Labs and Latitude (all new backers), as well as General Catalyst, Oak HC/FT, Tiger Global, Target Global, Durable Capital, Tal Capital and Entrée Capital (all previous backers) also participating. Other past investors, notably,  include another major player in the world of API-based financial services, Stripe.
 As with other companies in categories that have seen a huge surge of demand in the last year, financial services — and in particular those providing services to be able to carry out transactions online via the internet or phone — have proven to be some of the most mandatory and most used. (And no wonder, since bills still need paying, food and other items still need to be purchased, loans very much still need to be made and so on.)
 This was what many would call an “opportunistic” raise, made not to keep the lights on or to extend runway, but because the money was being offered to Rapyd at good terms, and there were smart places where it could be put to use to grow the business.
 “We didn’t plan to raise money when we raised this round, but when the pandemic came in our business started to boom,” Shtilman said. “We were approached by existing investors to scale beyond our original business plans after we completed our 2021 growth plans in three months in 2020. So we thought the timing was probably right for world domination.”
 Shtilman was partly (only partly) joking — he has a sort of deadpan delivery that I can’t quite capture here — but it’s a far cry from the startup’s early days, when “no one wanted to invest because e veryone thought it would be too hard to execute.  Even our early investors advised us to focus on a smaller concept. But we thought building globally doesn’t work. To start small is against the idea. Over the last several years, the need to explain what we do [has] almost vanished.”
 The challenge (and opportunity) that Rapyd identified back in 2017 when it first opened for business (having been hatched in Israel originally) is that the global commerce and financial markets are very highly fragmented. Consumers and businesses in individual markets have their own preferred payment methods and demands, regulations differ and the key companies involved vary from country to country.
 Meanwhile, APIs have long been a great instrument for integration and connection: Using a few lines of code — and presuming your own services are built on code too — you can knit together services, and bring in commoditized functionality that would take ages to build from the ground up, cutting down the effort and work needed, to focus on making your core business more unique.
  While companies like Stripe, Twilio and many others had identified the opportunity of leveraging APIs to scale out a world of functionality to a wider set of would-be customers, what Rapyd really identified and built out was the idea of loading not just one, two or three services, but hundreds (even thousands) of features into that proposition. In fintech, where those services are complex, there is a big array of them from which to choose what to build, and also a big pool of would-be customers to use them, if you are aiming wide.
 The idea is smart and, as Shtilman noted, very much in keeping with the economies of scale that exist in e-commerce and fintech: Individual transactions are at the end of the day very incremental, so services that bring many together can finally start to conceive of interesting returns.
 That, of course, is not just something Rapyd has identified and run with. That is to say, the company has a number of competitors now in the market.
 Just last week, Germany-based Mambu, which also provides an API-based suite of services (7,000 at last count) under the idea of “banking as a service,”  raised $135 million at a valuation of over $2 billion.  Stripe, a backer of Rapyd, also has continued to expand and add a number of services well beyond payments.  Thought Machine also raised a big round last year;  Temenos and Italy’s  Edera are also strong players here.
 And the field has so much opportunity that it’s even attracting a lot of newer entrants: witness  Unit, another interesting player that  came out of stealth in the U.S. in December with an interesting list of backers of its own.
  “To build financial infrastructure, it doesn’t matter whether you are a small mom and pop or something bigger, you need many things, and if you want to sell in more than one jurisdiction you need a lot of those services,” Shtilman noted about the need for scale and breadth in a fintech platform proposition. He’s also very sanguine about competition.
 “They have emerged like mushrooms after the rain,” he said. “But if you don’t have competition it means you don’t have a business, so this is good. It means there is a lot of demand. But for now we are the market leader. We think we will become the AWS of this space.”
  “The payment landscape varies dramatically across countries. A company doing business globally might need to accept hundreds of local payment methods. Rapyd’s API, which abstracts away this complexity, is currently powering what we think are many of the world’s most exciting companies,” said Kris Fredrickson, managing partner at Coatue, in a statement. “We are honored to partner with Arik and team for the next phase of the Rapyd journey.”
title: Rapyd，提供基于API的“金融科技即服务”平台，以$ 2.5B的融资后估值筹集了3亿美元的D轮融资
title_en: Rapyd, which provides an API-based “fintech-as-a-service” platform, raises $300M Series D at a $2.5B post-money valuation

note:
据《商业内幕》报道，据报道，GitHub解雇了一名犹太雇员，原因是他在Slack上发布了一条消息，称“保持安全的兄弟关系，纳粹分子正在袭击美国国会大厦。”
 该消息引发了公司内部的争议，一位同事批评他使用分歧性语言。 GitHub的人力资源团队谴责该员工在公司的Slack频道中使用“纳粹”一词。两天后，据称GitHub以模糊的行为方式将他开除。该员工要求Business Insider保持匿名，以免担心受到网上骚扰。
 此举表明，GitHub管理部门缺乏对美国国会大厦遭到袭击的严重性以及纳粹（Nazi）一词的上下文使用的了解。在《商业内幕》（Business Insider）发布报告后，该公司在Twitter上因试图“两面”而受到广泛批评。
 现在，GitHub的1,700名员工中大约有200名已经签署了一封信，要求高管们采取更坚决的立场反对反犹太主义和白人至上。他们还想知道为什么解雇该雇员。
 GitHub的首席执行官Nat Friedman告诉工人，“该公司上周正在积极调查有关员工离职的情况，并将在进行彻底调查后采取任何适当的行动。”
 该员工表示，他之所以发布此消息，是因为他担心住在华盛顿特区的亲戚和同事。他的家人在大屠杀中丧生，并且看到一些暴徒与新纳粹组织有关。
 GitHub是开发人员平台GitHub，于2018年被Microsoft收购。在袭击美国国会大厦的那天，微软总裁布拉德·史密斯（Brad Smith）转推了一份声​​明，呼吁特朗普总统“制止混乱，促进和平的权力移交” 。”微软还表示将暂停政治捐款，就像Facebook和亚马逊一样。 
在停用其Slack帐户之前，该员工对收到的反吹感到困惑。 “我不知道，作为一个犹太人，说这个词会如此两极分化，”他在一个针对犹太雇员的Slack频道中说。 “我们从小说[纳粹]。 这是一个我们要讲述的故事，因为我们必须这样做-整个血统的抽取都掌握在获得该头衔的人们的手中。”  这是GitHub近年来第二次面对来自员工的重大批评。 2019年，超过150名工人签署了一封信，要求该公司取消与移民和海关执法局的20万美元合同。 它还紧随Twitter和Google等公司最近的员工积极行动之后。 去年12月，Google解雇了AI伦理学家Timnit Gebru，引发了整个硅谷科技工作者的谴责。
note_en:
GitHub reportedly fired a Jewish employee after he posted a message in Slack that said “stay safe homies, Nazis are about” the day of the attack on the US Capitol,  according to  Business Insider.
 The message sparked controversy inside the company, with one colleague criticizing him for using divisive language. GitHub’s HR team chastised the employee for using the word “Nazi” in a company Slack channel. Two days later, GitHub allegedly fired him, citing vague patterns of behavior. The employee asked  Business Insider to remain anonymous for fear of online harassment.
 The move shows a lack of understanding on the part of GitHub management, both about the gravity of the attack on the US Capitol and the contextual use of the word Nazi. After  Business Insider published its report, the company was widely criticized on Twitter for trying to “both sides” the issue.
 Now, roughly 200 of GitHub’s 1,700 employees have signed a letter asking executives to take a stronger stance against anti-Semitism and white supremacy. They also want to know why the employee was fired.
 GitHub CEO Nat Friedman told workers that “the company is actively looking into the circumstances surrounding the separation of an employee last week and will take any and all appropriate action following a thorough investigation.”
 The employee said he posted the message because he was concerned for his relatives and co-workers living in Washington, DC. He had family who died in the Holocaust and saw that some of the rioters were associated with neo-Nazi organizations.
 GitHub, a developer platform, was acquired by Microsoft in 2018. The day of the attack on the US Capitol,  Microsoft President Brad Smith retweeted a statement calling on President Trump to “put an end to the chaos and to facilitate the peaceful transition of power.”  Microsoft also said it would pause political contributions, much like Facebook and Amazon.
 Before his Slack account was deactivated, the employee expressed bewilderment at the blowback he’d received from his message. “I did not know that, as a Jew, it would be so polarizing to say this word,” he said in a Slack channel for Jewish employees. “We grew up saying [Nazi]. It was a story we told because we had to— the decimation of whole lines of ancestry were at the hands of people who went by that title.”
 This is the second time GitHub has faced significant criticism from employees in recent years. In 2019,  more than 150 workers signed a letter asking the company to cancel a $200,000 contract with Immigration and Customs Enforcement. It also comes on the heels of recent employee activism at companies like Twitter and Google. In December,  Google fired AI ethicist Timnit Gebru, prompting a wave of condemnation from tech workers across Silicon Valley.
title: 报告：GitHub解雇了一名在美国国会暴动期间在内部Slack中使用“纳粹”一词的员工； 约200名员工签署了一封信，质疑解雇
title_en: Report: GitHub fired an employee who used the term “Nazis” in an internal Slack during the US Capitol riot; ~200 staff signed a letter questioning the firing

note:
Google表示，它正在调查使用Android暴露通知系统跟踪COVID-19传播的Android应用程序存在的问题。一个问题开始引起使用该系统的应用程序的延迟，从而导致更长的加载时间和定期曝光检查的通知延迟。受此Android问题影响，英格兰和威尔士各地使用的NHS COVID-19应用受到影响，许多用户的通知区域都被加载了通知。
 谷歌发言人在周三早些时候的一份声明中告诉《 The Verge》：“我们知道一个问题会影响使用Android暴露通知系统开发的Android应用程序。” “我们的工程师正在调查问题。”
 截至周三下午，Google告诉The Verge，它已发布修复程序，但问题可能需要一些时间才能解决，尽管Google表示，如果问题持续存在，它将直接与任何受影响的应用开发者合作以解决该问题。
 谷歌发言人在一份声明中说：“ 1月12日晚，Android上的Exposure Notifications System出现了一个问题，开始导致对安装了这些应用程序的用户的潜在风险进行检查的延迟。” “我们已经发布了修复程序。设备追赶可能需要几个小时，在某些情况下，我们将直接与开发人员合作以帮助恢复。该问题并未造成任何数据丢失或潜在风险。”
 Google对此问题也做了一些澄清。该公司告诉The Verge问题在于签名密钥配置，这意味着曝光通知应用程序遇到了从其密钥服务器下载诊断密钥的问题。这导致通知延迟，这是一个严重的问题，因为在阻止任何积极的COVID-19病例扩散时，及时的联系人追踪工作很重要。修复建议发布时，Google建议用户不要清除应用程序中的存储空间。
   这些问题已影响到全球所有使用Android Exposure Notifications系统的应用程序。荷兰的CoronaMelder应用程序也遇到了问题。九月份，英格兰和威尔士有超过一千万的人下载了NHS COVID-19应用程序，但目前尚不清楚总下载量是多少，还是iOS和Android之间的划分。
 Google于2020年4月首次推出了其用于Android的冠状病毒跟踪系统。该应用程序使用蓝牙匿名广播数据，并帮助在全球范围内实现跟踪程序。 
1月13日10:18 AM更新：文章更新了，CoronaMelder应用程序的详细信息也遇到了问题。  美国东部时间1月13日下午2:45更新：添加了Google的新声明，称该公司已推出解决方案以解决延迟问题。 标题也已更新以反映这一事实。
note_en:
Google says it’s working to investigate issues with Android apps that use the Android Exposure Notifications System to track the spread of COVID-19. An issue began causing delays for apps using the system, resulting in longer load times and delays in notifications for regular exposure checks. The NHS COVID-19 app that’s used across England and Wales is  affected by this Android issue, with a loading notification stuck in the notifications area for many users.
 “We are aware of an issue affecting Android apps developed using the Android Exposure Notifications System,” a Google spokesperson told  The Verge in a statement earlier on Wednesday. “Our engineers are investigating the issue.”
 As of Wednesday afternoon, Google tells  The Verge it’s issued a fix, but it may take some time for issues to subside, though Google says it’ll work with any affected app developers directly to remedy the issue if it persists.
 “Late in the evening on January 12, an issue with the Exposure Notifications System on Android began causing delays in the checking of potential exposures for those with apps installed,” a Google spokesperson said in a statement. “We have issued a fix. It may take a few hours for devices to catch up, and in some cases we will work directly with developers to help with recovery. The issue did not cause the loss of any data or potential exposures.”
 Google also provided some clarity on the issue. The company tells  The Verge the issue was with the signature key configuration, which meant exposure notification apps ran into issues downloading diagnosis keys from their key servers. This resulted in notification delays, a serious issue given the important of timely contact tracing efforts in stemming the spread of any positive COVID-19 cases. Google advises users not clear their storage in the app while the fix is going out.
   These issues have been affecting all apps worldwide that use the Android Exposure Notifications System. The CoronaMelder app in the Netherlands is also  experiencing issues. More than 10 million people in England and Wales downloaded the NHS COVID-19 app  in September, but it’s not clear how many total downloads have been made to date or the split between iOS and Android.
 Google  first introduced its coronavirus tracking system for Android in April 2020. The app uses Bluetooth to anonymously broadcast data and help enable tracking and tracing programs worldwide.
 Update January 13th 10:18AM : Article updated with details on the CoronaMelder app also experiencing issues.
 Update January 13th, 2:45PM ET:  Added a new statement from Google saying the company has pushed out a fix to solve the delays. The headline has also been updated to reflect this fact.
title: 谷歌表示，它已经发布了针对COVID-19的Android曝光通知系统的修复程序； 全球用户报告的应用程序速度明显下降
title_en: Google says it has issued a fix for Android's Exposure Notifications System for COVID-19; users worldwide reported apps had slowed down significantly

note:
几个月来，Nextdoor主持人一直在努力解决其附近站点QAnon内容的挑战-但是在上周对国会大厦的致命攻击之后，主持人和公司政策小组之间的压力可能已经达到了一个临界点。
 据The Verge获得的论坛截图显示，至少从十月份开始，版主就一直在要求Nextdoor禁止QAnon内容。上周，Nextdoor的主持人开始直接在全国潜在顾客论坛（该网站是主持人的私人论坛）上对公司施加压力。在由The Verge获得的论坛帖子的屏幕截图中，主持人对Nextdoor的错误信息政策并未完全阻止QAnon等阴谋论的讨论表示关注。
 上周在国会大厦举行的亲特朗普暴动之后，一个用户回到了早期的QAnon帖子中，写道：“我对此事有加。 1月8日。有政策吗？过去一周之后，我们需要一些。关于这三个月，我还写了一封电子邮件给《 Next Door Leadership》，但没有得到回应。”
 直到骚乱发生五天后，Nextdoor才最终对请求做出了回应，要求主持人重新审视公司关于暴力内容的政策。在这篇文章中，Nextdoor社区负责人Caty K.写道：“我要重申，更广泛的Nextdoor团队致力于平台上所有成员和社区的安全。”她继续说：“上周在美国国会大厦发生的暴力事件也不例外。”
 但是，一些Nextdoor主持人说，该公司的错误信息政策并不能有效地解决QAnon问题，也没有得到足够的交流来帮助社区应对这一阴谋。该公司的错误信息政策要求主持人举报散布“与选举和COVID-19有关的错误信息”的人，但并未直接针对QAnon等阴谋论。在对国会大厦的攻击之后，许多QAnon理论都带有煽动暴力的隐含风险，但主持人发现很难证明将其删除为直接暴力内容是合理的。同时，当前的《 Nextdoor缓和政策》不包括禁止讨论阴谋论的禁令。
 一位主持人在主题中写道：“问题在于，这项政策是针对选举和Covid-19信息而专门制定的，没有提及任何可用于政治误传和煽动社区恐惧的行为。”
 “ Facebook已经宣布它将自动删除带有'Stop The Steal'和#StopTheSteal短语的内容，”加州负责人Steve C.回应道。 “ Nextdoor计划这样做吗？” 
周一，Caty写道：“ Nextdoor将QAnon视为仇恨团体，”这是对名为“ FB禁止所有QAnon内容-ND政策是什么？”的主题的回应。 Caty继续说道：“如果您看到拥护这些意识形态的内容或成员，请向我们的团队报告，我们将处理。我知道我们没有可供大家参考的小组列表，我将努力使之更加清楚，但目前，此评论的目的是确认应删除QAnon内容。”
 周三，Nextdoor向The Verge确认将QAnon归类为仇恨团体。尽管如此，仍没有努力将QAnon政策传达给日常用户，并且截至发布之日，Nextdoor尚未在其网站上更新其错误信息政策以反映其将QAnon归类为仇恨团体的信息。 “目前我们还没有计划将其发送给[主持人]，” Caty在回应该帖子时表示，该决定是否会在论坛之外传达。
 Nextdoor也将The Verge告知了其错误信息和暴力内容政策。 “ Nextdoor上任何组织或呼吁暴力的帖子或内容都会被立即删除，” Nextdoor发言人告诉The Verge。 “ Nextdoor的邻里运营团队还结合使用了技术和成员报告，以主动识别和删除内容。
 过去，Nextdoor一直在努力建立清晰的节制政策。隔壁的社区主要是自治的，无偿的“社区领导”负责报告和删除其社区中的内容。这导致内容被错误地删除或保留下来。去年6月，The Verge报道说，Nextdoor主持人错误地撤下了支持黑人生活问题运动的帖子。
 十月，Recode报道说，在2020年美国总统大选前的最后几周，与QAnon相关的内容在平台上蓬勃发展。在一个例子中，Recode说，一个用户在Twitter上对Nextdoor进行了数周的轰炸，然后该平台删除了“包含QAnon谈话要点”的帖子。
 根据Nextdoor的规则，禁止在主要社区中讨论国家政治。结果，公共和私人团体已经成长为容纳这些讨论的场所。在The Verge获得的论坛帖子中，社区主持人对可能是暴力或极端主义帖子的私人团体表示担忧。
  “我们如何确保锁定的小组不参与有害的讨论？”亚利桑那州主持人詹妮弗五世在周二的论坛帖子中写道。 “我们有很多亲特朗普/爱国者团体，我担心。我还担心其他负责人或社区审核员会看到我报告这些团体和反对意见。” 
“我担心的是QAnon内容，以及其他带有阴谋论，暴力宣传等内容，这些内容属于*私人*小组，由于该小组的成员希望得到该内容，因此不会得到报道，” Carol C. ，一位科罗拉多主持人上周在论坛上写道。 “从那时起，我在公开的政治团体中看到了某些这类内容，”
note_en:
For months, Nextdoor moderators have struggled with the challenge of addressing QAnon content on its neighborhood sites — but after last week’s deadly attack on the Capitol, the pressure between moderators and the company’s policy team may have reached a breaking point.
 Moderators have been asking Nextdoor to impose a ban against QAnon content since at least October, according to forum screenshots obtained by  The Verge. Last week, Nextdoor moderators began pressuring the company directly in the National Leads Forum, a private forum for moderators on the site. In screenshots of forum posts, obtained by  The Verge, moderators expressed concern that Nextdoor’s misinformation policies did not fully bar discussions of conspiracy theories like QAnon.
 Following last week’s pro-Trump riot at the Capitol, one user returned to an early QAnon thread, writing, “I am bumping this up. It’s January 8th. Any policies yet? After the past week, we need some. I also wrote an email to Next Door Leadership about this three months ago and got no response.”
 It wasn’t until five days after the riot that Nextdoor finally responded to the request, referring moderators back to the company’s policy on violent content. In this post, Caty K., Nextdoor head of community, wrote, “I want to reiterate that the broader Nextdoor team is committed to the safety of all members and communities on the platform.” She continued, “The violent events that took place at the US Capitol last week are no exception.”
 But some Nextdoor moderators say that the company’s misinformation policies don’t meaningfully address QAnon, and haven’t been communicated well enough to help communities deal with the conspiracy. The company’s misinformation policy asks moderators to report individuals who distribute “misinformation related to the Election and COVID-19,” but does not directly address conspiracy theories like QAnon. After the attack on the Capitol, many QAnon theories carry an implicit risk of inciting violence, but moderators find it hard to justify their removal as straightforwardly violent content. At the same time, current Nextdoor moderation policies do not include a ban on discussions of conspiracy theories.
 “The problem is this policy is written so specific to election and Covid-19 information and does not mention any violation that can be used for things like misinformation around politics and inciting fear in the community,” one moderator wrote in the thread.
 “Facebook has announced that it will be automatically removing content with the phrase ‘Stop The Steal’ and #StopTheSteal,” Steve C., a California lead responded. “Does Nextdoor plan to do the same?”
 On Monday, Caty wrote that “Nextdoor views QAnon as a hate group,” as a response to a thread titled “FB has banned all QAnon Content - what is ND policy?” Caty continued, “If you see content or members advocating these ideologies, please report them to our team and we will handle. I recognize we do not have a list of groups available for you all to reference, and I will work on that to make things clearer, but for now this comment serves the purpose of confirming that QAnon content should be removed.”
 On Wednesday, Nextdoor confirmed to  The Verge that it classifies QAnon as a hate group. Still, there’s been no effort to communicate the QAnon policy to everyday users, and as of publication, Nextdoor has not updated its misinformation policies on its website to reflect its classification of QAnon as a hate group. “Right now we don’t have plans to email it out [to moderators,]” Caty said in response to a post asking if the decision would be communicated beyond the forum.
 Nextdoor also referred  The Verge to its misinformation and violent content policies. “Any post or content on Nextdoor that organizes or calls for violence will be immediately taken down,” a Nextdoor spokesperson told  The Verge. “Nextdoor’s Neighborhood Operations Team also uses a combination of technology and member reports to proactively identify and remove content.
 Nextdoor has struggled to establish clear moderation policies in the past. Nextdoor neighborhoods are primarily self-governed, and unpaid “community leads” are in charge of reporting and removing content in their communities. This has led to content being wrongfully being removed or allowed to stay up. Last June,   The Verge reported that posts supporting the Black Lives Matter movement were being wrongly taken down by Nextdoor moderators.
 In October,   Recode reported that QAnon-related content flourished on the platform in the last few weeks in the lead-up to the 2020 US presidential election. In one instance,  Recode said that a user bombarded Nextdoor for weeks on Twitter before the platform removed a post “containing QAnon talking points.”
 According to Nextdoor’s rules, discussions of national politics are banned on the main community feed. As a result, public and private groups have grown to house these discussions. In forum posts obtained by  The Verge, community moderators expressed worry over private groups that could be housing violent or extremist posts.
  “How can we ensure locked Groups are not participating in harmful discussions?” Jennifer V, an Arizona moderator, wrote in a forum post Tuesday. “We have a LOT of pro-Trump/Patriot Groups that I worry about. I also worry about other Leads or Community Reviewers seeing me report the Groups and the backlash.”
 “My concern is QAnon content, as well as other content with conspiracy theories, promotions of violence, etc., that is in *private* groups that won’t get reported because the members of the group WANT that content,” Carol C., a Colorado moderator wrote in the forums last week. “I saw some of this type of content in the public political groups that have since gone private.”
title: 一些主持人说，Nextdoor的策略不能充分解决QAnon，也不能很好地与用户沟通。 Nextdoor表示将QAnon归类为仇恨团体
title_en: Some moderators say Nextdoor's policies don't adequately address QAnon and aren't well communicated to users; Nextdoor says it classifies QAnon as a hate group
